skill.name: querying-logseq-data
skill.description: Guides users through building, explaining, and optimizing Datalog queries for Logseq DB graphs using Datascript patterns, pull syntax, aggregations, rules, and DB-specific attributes.
skill.summary: Build and refine Logseq Datalog queries for DB graphs.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, weak crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found Datalog and Logseq Datascript examples rather than executable shell or cryptographic code. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, malicious network use, or OS command execution was found. The remaining concern is low risk because the skill declares file and web-capable tools in its metadata.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Datalog False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged blocks are Clojure-style Logseq Datascript query examples. I found no shell interpreter, Ruby backtick execution, subprocess invocation, or user-controlled OS command execution in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is consistently documented as Logseq Datalog or Datascript query material. The reviewed lines are fenced examples, not instructions to execute operating system commands.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Findings Are Query Syntax False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged high and low severity lines use recursive query rules and Logseq attributes such as block UUID and database identifiers. I found no hashing API, weak cryptographic algorithm, host enumeration, or system reconnaissance command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms appear inside Datalog examples and optimization notes. Their surrounding context confirms graph traversal and indexed Logseq attributes, not cryptography or system probing.

low_findings.title: Broad Tool Permissions Require Marketplace Disclosure
low_findings.description: The skill metadata allows local file reading tools and web tools. This is not malicious by itself, but users should know the skill can read local workspace content and request external web resources when invoked.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration explicitly includes Read, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, and WebSearch. I found no evidence of exfiltration or forced network use, so the concern remains disclosure-level.

content.value_statement: Logseq DB graphs can be hard to query with raw Datalog syntax. This skill provides patterns, examples, and optimization guidance for Datascript queries.
actual_capabilities: Explains Logseq Datalog clauses such as find, in, where, and pull.
Provides query patterns for pages, blocks, tags, properties, tasks, and journals.
Shows pull syntax for selected attributes, nested references, reverse references, and limits.
Covers aggregations, rules, negation, or clauses, and recursive graph traversal.
Translates simple Logseq query ideas into full Datalog query structure.
Suggests optimization techniques such as selective clauses and indexed attributes.
limitations: It does not execute Logseq database queries by itself.
Examples focus on Logseq DB graphs, not every markdown graph behavior.
Custom properties require the user to provide names, types, and expected values.
Result transforms and custom views still need manual testing inside Logseq.

use_cases.title: Build Task Dashboards
use_cases.description: Create queries that find tasks by status, priority, deadline, tag, or journal date.

use_cases.title: Convert Simple Queries
use_cases.description: Turn simple Logseq query UI filters into structured Datalog with clear clauses.

use_cases.title: Optimize Graph Queries
use_cases.description: Review slow or broad queries and improve filters, pull attributes, and result shape.
use_cases.target_user: Plugin developers and knowledge graph maintainers

prompt_templates.title: Find Tagged Pages
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a Logseq Datalog query that finds pages tagged with [tag name] and returns their titles.

prompt_templates.title: Filter By Properties
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Logseq Datalog query for blocks with class [class name], property [property name], and value [value]. Explain each clause.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize With Aggregation
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Logseq Datalog query that groups [content type] by [property] and counts or averages [metric]. Include notes about result shape.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Recursive Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this recursive Logseq Datalog query for descendants or references. Improve performance and limit the returned attributes: [paste query].

output_examples.input: Find all high priority tasks that are still in progress.
output_examples.output: Returns a query plan that filters task blocks by class, priority, and status, then explains which attributes are used.

output_examples.input: Show books grouped by author with a count for each author.
output_examples.output: Provides an aggregation approach, describes the expected grouped rows, and notes how missing author values affect results.

output_examples.input: My query is slow because it pulls every attribute from many blocks.
output_examples.output: Suggests narrower pull attributes, more selective filters first, and limits for nested child or reference data.

best_practices: Provide the exact class names, property names, and expected value formats before asking for a query.
Ask for an explanation of each clause when learning or debugging a query.
Limit pulled attributes when querying large graphs or nested references.
anti_patterns: Do not assume markdown graph attributes are identical to DB graph attributes.
Do not use broad wildcard pulls when only titles or selected properties are needed.
Do not skip testing result transforms and custom views in Logseq.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps write and explain Datalog queries for Logseq DB graphs.

faq.question: Can it run queries against my Logseq graph?
faq.answer: No. It provides query guidance and examples, but it does not execute database queries.

faq.question: Does it support Datascript syntax?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples focus on Logseq DB graphs and the Datascript query engine.

faq.question: Can it help with query performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It can suggest selective clauses, indexed attributes, narrower pull patterns, and result limits.

faq.question: Can it convert simple Logseq queries to Datalog?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for translating simple query UI patterns into Datalog structure.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide class names, property names, sample values, expected output, and any query that needs debugging.
